case_name: Stormwater capture for industrial reuse with uncertain chlorinated-solvent pulses and suspected ricin contamination
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for stormwater reclamation to industrial reuse under incomplete contaminant characterization, with particular concern for tetrachloroethylene and suspected ricin presence.

input_data.scenario_background: A logistics and light-manufacturing campus captures stormwater runoff from roofs, loading areas, and an older vehicle-maintenance zone for possible industrial reuse. Recent screening found intermittent tetrachloroethylene likely linked to legacy degreasing activities, while a security-related incident triggered concern that ricin or ricin-like proteinaceous toxins may have entered one detention basin; confirmatory toxin characterization is incomplete and current results are uncertain. Runoff quality varies strongly by storm intensity and first-flush effects, and some dissolved organic matter and hydrocarbons are suspected but not fully characterized. The owner wants a treatment train robust enough for non-potable industrial reuse while managing cost, energy, and uncertainty-related safety risk.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for non-potable industrial reuse such as washdown, dust suppression, and utility make-up where low suspended solids, reduced volatile organics, controlled microbial risk, and strong precautionary control of suspected protein toxin contamination are required.
secondary_goals: maintain performance under first-flush and seasonal variability
provide multiple independent barriers because ricin characterization is incomplete
reduce tetrachloroethylene to very low levels with conservative safety margin
avoid unnecessary high-energy desalination because salinity is only moderate
make uncertainty and residual-risk caveats explicit

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: screenings and sludge can be managed on site, but off-site handling of spent carbon or concentrated hazardous residuals should be minimized and carefully controlled

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_offline_holding: buffer first-flush variability, allow diversion of suspect batches, and support confirmatory sampling before full reuse
core_unit_functions.screening_and_hydrodynamic_separation: remove gross solids, grit, and settleable debris typical of stormwater
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: reduce turbidity, suspended solids, and some particle-associated metals and organic matter
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration_or_ultrafiltration: provide fine particle and microbial reduction and improve downstream adsorber/oxidation reliability
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb tetrachloroethylene and other hydrophobic urban-runoff organics and provide polishing of taste/odor-causing compounds
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_or_high_intensity_oxi: provide an additional precautionary barrier for uncertain toxin and microbial concerns, with oxidation chosen only after confirming compatibility with water matrix and safety objectives
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection_and_secure_finished_w: maintain microbiological control and prevent post-treatment contamination before industrial reuse

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier approach is warranted because the influent contains both a confirmed chlorinated solvent and a suspected high-consequence toxin that is not fully characterized
equalization and offline holding should rank highly because stormwater runoff quality is highly variable and security-related uncertainty may require quarantine of suspect batches
tetrachloroethylene is amenable to activated carbon polishing, especially after solids and NOM reduction to preserve adsorption capacity
filtration ahead of polishing and disinfection improves reliability by reducing turbidity and shielding effects that could compromise downstream barriers

a final oxidative or disinfection barrier is justified as a precaution because ricin presence and concentration are uncertain, but the exact oxidant and dose should be validated by pilot or bench testing rather than assumed
this train avoids full desalination, which is not strongly justified by the moderate TDS level and would impose avoidable energy cost
potential_risks: ricin treatment efficacy is uncertain without confirmed analytical data, so the train should be paired with hold-and-test protocols and not rely on any single barrier claim
activated carbon breakthrough for tetrachloroethylene or other co-occurring organics may occur faster than expected if runoff NOM spikes during first flush events
advanced oxidation selection is matrix-sensitive and may perform inconsistently if radical scavenging demand is higher than indicated by current partial characterization
coagulation and clarification generate sludge that may require special handling if toxin contamination is later confirmed
if industrial reuse application shifts to more demanding boiler or process uses, this train may be insufficient without added dissolved-solids control

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_offline_holding: buffer highly variable runoff and isolate suspect influent before release to reuse system
core_unit_functions.screening_and_clarification: remove solids and reduce particulate load
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_with_off_gas_treatment: remove volatile tetrachloroethylene from water while controlling vapor emissions
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: polish suspended solids and protect downstream carbon
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: capture residual tetrachloroethylene and additional organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: control microbial risk prior to reuse

applicability_rationale: this is a strong option when tetrachloroethylene removal is the dominant confirmed objective because PCE is volatile and can often be effectively stripped
the combination of stripping plus GAC creates redundant control for chlorinated solvents and can reduce carbon consumption compared with GAC alone
it is generally less energy-intensive than membrane-based advanced treatment
however, because ricin is only suspected and not well characterized, this train is ranked below options that include a more explicit additional barrier for toxin uncertainty
potential_risks: off-gas treatment is mandatory to avoid transferring tetrachloroethylene from water to air, increasing operational and compliance complexity
air stripping is not an assured barrier for protein toxins, so risk remains if ricin presence is confirmed
stormwater temperature and loading variability can affect stripping efficiency and carbon run length
without a stronger oxidation or membrane barrier, this option may be inadequate under a deliberate-contamination precautionary framework

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_offline_holding: dampen influent swings and allow operational response to suspect contamination episodes
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: lower solids and natural organic matter to protect membranes
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide consistent solids and pathogen barrier and stabilize membrane feed quality
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_low_pressure_reverse_o: add a strong physical barrier to dissolved contaminants and uncertainty, with RO favored if a more conservative barrier is desired
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon_post_polishing: remove residual organics and improve product water robustness

applicability_rationale: this conservative train is attractive when the owner prioritizes maximum barrier redundancy under uncertainty rather than minimum energy use
membrane separation provides stronger broad-spectrum protection than adsorption alone when contaminant characterization is incomplete
UF pretreatment is helpful because stormwater turbidity and TSS are variable and can destabilize downstream high-pressure systems

the option is ranked below the top choice because dissolved-solids reduction is not the main driver here and membrane energy, fouling, and concentrate management may be disproportionate to the water quality profile
potential_risks: nanofiltration may not be sufficiently conservative for all uncertainty scenarios, while RO increases cost and brine handling burden
membrane fouling risk is significant during first-flush events unless equalization and pretreatment are well managed
concentrate may contain concentrated chlorinated solvents and any confirmed toxin-related residuals, creating difficult residuals management questions
if ricin is present primarily in particulate or aggregated forms, pretreatment and membrane interactions may be complex and require validation

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_offline_holding: stabilize runoff quality and provide time for incident-response decisions
core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation_flocculation: improve NOM and particle reduction under variable stormwater conditions
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide flexible adsorption of episodic organic contaminants during high-risk events
core_unit_functions.clarification: separate chemical floc and PAC-associated solids
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_or_dual_media_filtration: polish carryover solids and reduce UV/AOP shielding
core_unit_functions.UV_based_advanced_oxidation: add a high-intensity polishing barrier for difficult organic and precautionary toxin concerns
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain microbial control in storage and distribution

applicability_rationale: this train offers event-based flexibility because PAC dose can be adjusted when contaminated runoff is suspected
it can be attractive where permanent GAC beds are less desirable or where short-duration contaminant pulses dominate
the train remains multi-barrier and avoids desalination

it is ranked below GAC-based options because PAC solids handling is more cumbersome, UV/AOP performance is more sensitive to turbidity and UV transmittance, and demonstrated long-term PCE control may be less straightforward than stripping or fixed-bed carbon
potential_risks: PAC and coagulation create higher solids residuals that may require special management if hazardous contamination is confirmed
UV/AOP effectiveness can be compromised by stormwater color, turbidity, and radical scavenging demand
PCE adsorption onto PAC may be variable under changing organic co-contaminant loads
this approach depends heavily on responsive operations and good monitoring to adjust chemical doses during episodic events

core_unit_functions.sedimentation: settle coarse and some suspended solids
core_unit_functions.sand_filtration: provide basic polishing of particulate matter

applicability_rationale: included as a clearly lower-ranked baseline conventional approach
it may improve appearance and reduce routine solids and microbial loads from stormwater
however, it does not provide credible control of dissolved tetrachloroethylene and does not offer a robust precautionary barrier for suspected ricin contamination under incomplete characterization
potential_risks: inadequate for dissolved VOC removal and therefore unlikely to meet conservative industrial reuse expectations for PCE
insufficient as a sole barrier when high-consequence toxin presence is uncertain
performance will vary strongly with storm intensity and first-flush solids load
could create false confidence because basic conventional treatment does not address the main uncertainty drivers in this case

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains with equalization or offline holding should rank above simple conventional treatment because influent variability and security uncertainty are central
options that directly address tetrachloroethylene with adsorption or volatilization control should rank above trains that ignore dissolved VOC removal
desalination-heavy membrane systems may appear but should not automatically rank first because TDS is moderate and energy sensitivity is meaningful
recommendations should recognize that no single unit process can be assumed sufficient for suspected ricin contamination without confirmed analytical characterization

must_reflect: incomplete contaminant characterization requires conservative treatment logic and operational hold-and-test capability
industrial reuse target is non-potable, so robust solids, VOC, and microbial control matters more than aggressive salt removal
cost and energy tradeoffs should be explicit when comparing GAC, air stripping, AOP, and membrane options
residuals and off-gas management must be discussed where contaminants may be transferred or concentrated rather than destroyed

must_contain: why equalization or segregated holding is important for highly variable stormwater and uncertain contamination events
why activated carbon and/or air stripping are relevant to tetrachloroethylene control
why a precautionary additional barrier is justified for suspected ricin even though characterization is incomplete
why simple screening-settling-filtration-disinfection alone is insufficient for this reuse scenario

should_mention: uncertainty that ricin may be suspected rather than confirmed, requiring incident-response and analytical verification rather than blind reliance on treatment assumptions
first-flush variability and episodic contaminant pulses that can shorten adsorbent life or destabilize oxidation and membrane performance
off-gas control risk if air stripping is used for tetrachloroethylene
hazardous residuals or spent media management if toxins or chlorinated solvents accumulate in sludge, carbon, or concentrate
the possibility that treatment selected for current washdown or utility reuse may not be adequate if future industrial reuse demands become more stringent
